ontology.foodex2_description: Crackers and crispbreads — fish-based cracker analogue; closest FoodEx2 match under snack/cracker category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for Keropok Ikan specifically; A0EJW (crackers) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Malaysian snack food.

main_ingredients: minced fish (ikan kembung or ikan tamban)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Keropok Ikan is approximately 30g (about 8–10 crackers), providing roughly 145 kcal, 3.75g protein, 6.84g fat, and 17.55g carbohydrates.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Foods — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database (Crackers and Snacks category)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Keropok Ikan adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for fish-based crackers and snacks. IMR Malaysia MyFCD consulted as secondary reference for Malaysian fish cracker composition. Values are consistent with the input enriched_json record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9). Saturated fat estimated at approximately 4.5g/100g based on typical palm oil deep-frying composition per HPB and USDA SR Legacy data for fried fish crackers. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in primary sources for this preparation. Cholesterol estimated at 25mg/100g based on fish content per USDA FoodData Central (fish cracker analogue). Water content set to -1 as moisture data for deep-fried keropok not reported in primary sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on tapioca/sago starch-based crackers
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Keropok Ikan in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 estimated based on analogous starch-based crackers (tapioca/sago flour base, deep-fried) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Tapioca-based crackers (e.g., prawn crackers, shrimp crackers) have reported GI values of 67–74; 70 used as central estimate. Fish protein and frying oil moderately reduce glycemic response but high starch density dominates. Glycemic load per 30g serving = (70 × 17.55g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 12.3.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit Keropok Ikan intake; a single 30g serving provides approximately 11% of the 2,300mg daily sodium limit, and typical snacking portions can easily exceed this, significantly elevating blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 70) and high refined starch content (58.5g carbohydrate/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Keropok Ikan due to its high GI (70) and high refined tapioca/sago starch content, which causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, limit to no more than 15g and pair with a protein or fibre source to blunt glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation with elevated total fat (22.8g/100g), saturated fat (~4.5g/100g), and high sodium (850mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Keropok Ikan only occasionally and in small portions, as the combination of deep-frying, elevated saturated fat, and high sodium contributes to dyslipidaemia and hypertension risk factors associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory potential from deep-frying oils and refined starch
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: While Keropok Ikan provides some fish-derived protein and trace omega-3 fatty acids, the deep-frying process and refined starch base may promote inflammation; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume in moderation and prefer baked or air-fried alternatives where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Keropok Ikan, literally 'fish crackers' in Malay, is a traditional Malaysian snack food deeply associated with the East Coast states of Terengganu and Kelantan. It is made by combining minced fish — typically ikan kembung (Indian mackerel) or ikan tamban (sardine) — with sago flour or tapioca flour, water, salt, and a small amount of sugar. The mixture is shaped into cylindrical logs, steamed until cooked through, then sliced into thin rounds and sun-dried until hard and brittle. The dried rounds are then deep-fried in hot oil, causing them to puff up dramatically into light, airy, and exceptionally crispy crackers with a pronounced umami-rich fish flavour and a satisfying crunch. Per 100g, Keropok Ikan provides approximately 485 kcal, 12.5g protein, 22.8g fat, and 58.5g carbohydrates, with a high sodium content of 850mg. Its high glycemic index (estimated GI 70) and refined starch base make it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The snack is enjoyed on its own or as a crunchy accompaniment to traditional rice dishes such as Nasi Kerabu and Nasi Lemak. It is a culturally significant street food and festive snack across Malaysia and is widely available in Malaysian wet markets, roadside stalls, and supermarkets.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fish crackers?
What is the GI of Keropok Ikan?
What fish is used in Keropok Ikan?
meal_context: crunchy side dish for rice meals

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore food composition data for fish-based crackers and snacks (adapted match), consistent with the input enriched_json record. GI is an analogous estimate based on tapioca/sago starch crackers from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Keropok Ikan was found. Saturated fat is estimated from typical deep-frying oil composition. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact HPB or IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for Keropok Ikan (deep-fried) was directly matched by name.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for deep-fried Keropok Ikan not available in HPB Singapore or IMR Malaysia MyFCD databases as of 2024. 2. trans_fat_g set to -1: trans fat content not reported in primary sources for this preparation; likely low given typical Malaysian frying oil use but not confirmed. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture content for deep-fried keropok not reported in primary sources consulted. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero for this deep-fried starch-and-fish product; no plant-based vitamin C sources present. 5. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on tapioca/sago starch-based crackers from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021); no direct GI measurement for Keropok Ikan was found. 6. Saturated fat of 4.5g/100g is estimated from typical palm oil deep-frying composition per HPB and USDA SR Legacy data. 7. Cholesterol of 25mg/100g estimated from fish content per USDA FoodData Central fish cracker analogue. 8. This is a Malaysian dish; multilingual coverage follows Malay cuisine rules (Malay name mandatory). Chinese script aliases provided for regional Chinese-Malaysian community recognition. Tamil and Hindi local names not applicable for this East Coast Malay traditional snack. 9. Input provenance was ai_generated with confidence 0.9; values have been cross-checked against HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central for plausibility.
